title: Fakin It

jokes.text: Well, they've got one guy who won't be talking. That is, unless there's a hand inside of him.
jokes.setup: Michael says they have a witness who 'won't be talking' — implying a cooperative witness or a dead one
jokes.punchline: Unless there's a hand inside of him — revealing he means Franklin, the puppet
jokes.explanation: Elegant misdirection — the 'won't be talking' framing leads the audience to assume a human witness, and the puppet reveal recontextualizes it perfectly. Strong economy; wastes no words.

jokes.text: Oh, please, Michael. Even then, I wouldn't say anything.
jokes.setup: Michael says the witness won't talk 'unless there's a hand inside of him' (meaning Franklin the puppet)
jokes.punchline: Lucille, mishearing or not caring to distinguish, deadpans that she personally wouldn't talk even under those conditions
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly in-character misread. Lucille's steely refusal to ever cooperate is so total that she applies it even to being a literal puppet. It only works because of the specific character — no one else in the family delivers this line with the same chilling plausibility.

jokes.text: Yes! I got him in the system. He is re-dyed, rested and ready to work.
jokes.setup: The witness list is being discussed as a serious legal matter
jokes.punchline: Gob has entered his puppet Franklin as a legal witness and describes him like a refreshed athlete returning from injury
jokes.explanation: 'Re-dyed, rested and ready to work' treats the puppet with the same dignity as a professional athlete, which is peak Gob delusion. Character-specific absurdism that reveals how seriously Gob takes Franklin as a person.

jokes.text: I gotta tell Franklin. / I think he meant 'get Franklin'.
jokes.setup: Gob says he has to 'tell' Franklin the news
jokes.punchline: Michael gently corrects that he meant 'get Franklin' — implying Gob is treating the puppet as a separate entity who must be informed
jokes.explanation: A quick, precise joke about Gob's dissociation from reality regarding Franklin. Michael's correction is the straight-man voice of reason doing exactly what it should — exposing absurdity with minimal words.

jokes.text: Franklin, guess what? / But I don't know anything.
jokes.setup: Gob runs to 'tell' Franklin the news
jokes.punchline: Franklin immediately preemptively denies knowing anything — a witness-list joke, but also Gob performing both sides of a conversation
jokes.explanation: Franklin's preemptive denial mirrors the actual family's strategy ('I don't know anything') but delivered through a puppet Gob is fully inhabiting. The layering — Franklin as both joke subject and comedic stand-in — is efficient.

jokes.text: He certainly hasn't heard anything from me. Mother doesn't know anything, either. / I don't know anything. / Well, you both certainly act innocent.
jokes.setup: Gob (as Franklin) and Lucille both volunteer that they know nothing
jokes.punchline: Michael's dry 'You both certainly act innocent' — implying they are obviously guilty
jokes.explanation: Solid irony — the overprotesting innocence is undercut perfectly by Michael's understated observation. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster knew a great deal. For Lucille, almost nightly, got drunk and told him.
jokes.setup: Characters insist no one in the family knows anything incriminating
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals Lucille regularly got drunk and told Buster everything — immediately undercutting the lie
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-omniscient-exposer is one of Arrested Development's signature tools, and this is a well-executed example. The rhythm of the reveal — 'almost nightly, got drunk and told him' — has a deadpan specificity that elevates it.

jokes.text: Your father says he wants me to go all the way to Fallujah. / I thought he meant the sex act that's so popular with your generation.
jokes.setup: Lucille mentions George Sr. asked her to go to Fallujah (Iraq, a war zone)
jokes.punchline: She thought he meant a sex act named 'Fallujah' — casually sexualizing warzone geography in a way only Lucille would
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Only Lucille Bluth would (a) casually know what 'Fallujah' is as an alleged sex act, (b) describe it as 'popular with your generation,' and (c) volunteer this to her child without shame. The dark juxtaposition of military geography and sexual slang, delivered with complete Lucille composure, is the show at its sharpest. High character integration — no other character delivers this line.

jokes.text: I know you're awake. Faker!
jokes.setup: Scene transition into Buster's hospital situation — Buster may be faking his coma
jokes.punchline: Lucille, with no preamble, calls out Buster as a 'faker' — establishing the episode's central theme and her total lack of maternal concern
jokes.explanation: The abruptness and certainty of 'Faker!' from a mother at her comatose son's bedside is textbook Lucille — cold, accusatory, and entirely plausible. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Whoa. I did not say that. / You did, Gob. Your mouth moved. Your hand didn't.
jokes.setup: Something racist/inflammatory is apparently said — implied to come from Franklin
jokes.punchline: Michael points out Gob's mouth moved but his hand (puppet) didn't — meaning Gob said it himself and tried to blame the puppet
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion of the ventriloquist premise — the joke about ventriloquism is normally 'the puppet's mouth moves.' Here, Gob has said something he can't own, so the mouth/hand logic is perfectly inverted to expose him. 'Well, I should work on that' is the cherry on top — Gob treats it as a technical craft problem, not a social one. One of the episode's sharpest constructions.

jokes.text: He, she, what's the difference? / Hear, hear. In the dark it all looks the same.
jokes.setup: Lucille tries to walk back her gendered slip about N. Bluth
jokes.punchline: George Sr. agrees — 'In the dark it all looks the same' — a deeply inappropriate sexual/gender comment played completely straight by both
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s '...in the dark it all looks the same' is gross and weirdly funny — it's played as both a deflection and an inadvertent window into his character. The 'Hear, hear' setup gives it a fake pomposity that makes the punchline land harder.

jokes.text: Not another media whore. / Like to see a whore that does that! Not the carrying the weight part, but, on the other hand, if it's free.
jokes.setup: Michael says Jan Eagleman 'carries a lot of weight' and 'is not charging us anything'
jokes.punchline: Lucille runs with the 'whore' metaphor she introduced, riffs on the weight-carrying and free service aspects in a deeply inappropriate double entendre
jokes.explanation: Lucille's ability to sustain a vulgar metaphor across multiple clauses is a distinct comedic skill. The structure is slightly bloated ('not the carrying the weight part') but 'on the other hand, if it's free' saves it.

jokes.text: You know, Michael, just because you played a role, doesn't make it real. Of course, Buster did grow up to be Captain Hook.
jokes.setup: George Sr. mocks Michael for thinking playing a lawyer in a school play qualifies him
jokes.punchline: Narrator points out that Buster (who played Captain Hook) did in fact grow up to be a hook-handed man — making the school-play-to-reality connection disturbingly literal
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments and a strong candidate for the season's best narrator joke. The logic is airtight: George Sr.'s point (school plays don't make reality) is immediately devastated by the reminder that Buster literally became his school-play character. It's a callback to a multi-season arc, uses the narrator's unique omniscient voice, and rewards loyal viewers enormously. The construction is perfect — statement, ironic contradiction, silence. Should be ranked among the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Not cool, Mom.
jokes.setup: Narrator drops the Captain Hook observation; Buster is present in the scene
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'Not cool, Mom' — addressed to Lucille rather than the narrator, as if she said it, and underreacting to the profound irony of being a hook-handed man who played Captain Hook
jokes.explanation: The misdirected blame (at Mom) and the casual underreaction to his own tragic irony ('not cool') is perfectly Buster. Short and precise.

jokes.text: Oh, no. He thinks he's a lawyer again. / Devotees will remember that Michael once played a lawyer in a school play.
jokes.setup: Michael says he can put together a good defense
jokes.punchline: Lucille's dismissive 'He thinks he's a lawyer again' + narrator's callback to the school play establish this as a recurring delusion
jokes.explanation: Solid setup for the school play joke that pays off brilliantly in joke #15. Works as character comedy on its own but is primarily scaffolding.

jokes.text: No one is going to lie on that stand. And no one is going to betray the family. / [Cut to: Buster rocking and humming nervously]
jokes.setup: Michael makes a solemn declaration about family unity and honesty
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to Buster rocking and humming — the narrator then explains this is because Buster is nervous, implying he knows things and is already betraying Michael's declaration
jokes.explanation: Classic AD juxtaposition cut — declaration immediately undercut by visual evidence to the contrary. The narrator's dry explanation adds another layer.

jokes.text: I'm doing a tech rehearsal for my new show, Judge Reinhold. / You know that there's an actor by that name, right?
jokes.setup: Jan introduces her show called 'Judge Reinhold'
jokes.punchline: Michael points out the famous actor shares that name — she's named a reality court show after a celebrity
jokes.explanation: AD's recurring love of real celebrity names used as absurdist comedy is on full display. The premise (a real judge named Reinhold) is funny on its own, and Michael's polite correction escalates it well.

jokes.text: I read somewhere Judge Judy made $25 million last year. And I'm like, 'Hey, I never even heard of the guy.'
jokes.setup: Jan explains her motivation for the Judge Reinhold show
jokes.punchline: She refers to 'Judge Judy' as a guy she never heard of — unaware (or not caring) that Judge Judy is a famous woman
jokes.explanation: Layered: she mistakes Judge Judy's gender AND doesn't know who she is despite having been inspired by her earnings. The obliviousness compounds on itself in a way that makes Jan a perfectly calibrated comic foil.

jokes.text: So, you're doing this because your name is Judge? / Honestly, this is the first time I put those two things together.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Jan's motivation was simply her name
jokes.punchline: She admits she's never connected her name 'Judge' with the concept of being a judge — despite creating a courtroom show called 'Judge Reinhold'
jokes.explanation: Perfect idiot logic. The joke works because it escalates Jan's obliviousness to an absurd extreme — she's built a whole career on the joke and only now connects the dots. 'This is the first time I put those two things together' is quotable and precisely calibrated.

jokes.text: Maybe we should take Judge out of the title, and go with my original title, Mr Reinhold's Courtroom. / No, no, no. We're changing the name.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests removing 'Judge' from the title to avoid actor confusion
jokes.punchline: Jan's original title 'Mr Reinhold's Courtroom' is even more confusingly identical to the actor's name; producer immediately shuts it down
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the Judge Reinhold bit — each attempt to fix the problem makes it worse. Good escalation comedy with a tidy producer reaction to cap it.

jokes.text: Got 250,000 talking magazine ads, and he wants to change the name.
jokes.setup: The name 'Judge Reinhold' is a disaster; Michael suggests changing it
jokes.punchline: Producer has already committed 250,000 'talking magazine ads' — the newly absurd technology introduced for this episode
jokes.explanation: The 'talking magazine ads' as a real technology in this universe is a recurring absurdist gag throughout the episode. This is its first appearance — funny as a concept, funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: We are going to need a defence attorney, right, for the mock trial? And that's not gonna be cheap. / You know the case. Why not you?
jokes.setup: Michael raises the need for a defense attorney as a cost issue
jokes.punchline: Jan suggests Michael himself — a non-lawyer whose only legal experience is a school play — serve as defense attorney
jokes.explanation: Pays off the school play setup and advances the plot simultaneously. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Yes. Yeah, it'll give me a chance to show my dad what I can do. / I'll show him. I'll make a fool out of him.
jokes.setup: Michael agrees to be the defense attorney to impress his father
jokes.punchline: Catches himself — his competitive instinct has him literally vowing to 'make a fool' of his own defendant/father, which is the opposite of good lawyering
jokes.explanation: A quick self-betrayal joke — Michael's competitive relationship with his father immediately corrupts his stated goal. The pause between 'show my dad what I can do' and 'I'll make a fool out of him' is the whole joke.

jokes.text: I just pray it's one of those things where he's unconscious throughout the entire trial, and when it's over, he wakes up and gets a big toy.
jokes.setup: Family rushes to hospital upon learning Buster is in a coma
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'prayer' is not for her son's health but that his unconsciousness lasts exactly as long as the trial — then she tacks on 'gets a big toy' as a token maternal afterthought
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Lucille moment. The prayer framing sets up pure maternal concern, then delivers the most coldly strategic wish possible (coma timed to the trial), then tosses in 'gets a big toy' as a tiny, wholly inadequate gesture toward motherhood. The toy is the kill shot — it's such a small, infantilizing consolation prize. Near-perfect character integration; only Lucille Bluth would construct this sentence.

jokes.text: Of course it crossed my mind, but this was all Buster.
jokes.setup: Michael accuses Lucille of putting Buster in a coma
jokes.punchline: Lucille doesn't deny the impulse — 'of course it crossed my mind' — just the execution. She considered it. She just didn't do it this time.
jokes.explanation: Three words — 'of course it crossed my mind' — do all the work. This is a confession of monstrous impulse delivered with complete composure, as if it's the most natural thing. Lucille character comedy at its sharpest. The 'but' pivot to blaming Buster is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: He got into your brother's pills. Not the Forget-Me-Nows? / He asked me for them. I didn't know. I thought he was dating again.
jokes.setup: Buster took Forget-Me-Nows (date rape drugs established earlier in the series) to forget his knowledge
jokes.punchline: Lucille gave them to him assuming he was dating — a callback to the drug's prior use as a date-rape facilitator, presented as casually as lending a cup of sugar
jokes.explanation: A deeply dark callback that depends entirely on show knowledge. The horror of Lucille casually handing her son roofies 'because she thought he was dating' is played with complete deadpan. Rewards loyal viewers with a gut-punch laugh.

jokes.text: Hope you don't mind me saying, but these things can go on indefinitely. My mother's been in that bed for two years. / How awful. / We don't have a private room. They won't let me take out her feeding tube.
jokes.setup: A sympathetic-looking man in the hospital waiting room offers condolences about Buster
jokes.punchline: His 'comfort' rapidly escalates into a disturbing TMI spiral about his own comatose mother, the lack of a private room, and his frustration at not being able to remove her feeding tube
jokes.explanation: The escalating overshare from a stranger is well-executed cringe comedy. Each new detail ('they won't let me take out her feeding tube') lands as darker and funnier.

jokes.text: And I'm lucky, I have the resources to pay for these medical bills. / You have resources? / What good is my money if I can't bring her back? / But you can't. / And money should be spent on dating, is that what you were gonna say?
jokes.setup: Hospital Man mentions he has resources; Lindsay (shallow, self-interested) perks up
jokes.punchline: He completes her unspoken mercenary thought ('money should be spent on dating') before she can say it — Lindsay's dating-for-money instinct is so obvious a stranger reads it
jokes.explanation: Character comedy at its best — Lindsay's shallow avarice is so legible that a stranger finishes her thought. 'And money should be spent on dating, is that what you were gonna say?' is the precise line, and it's a great one.

jokes.text: Let's go ahead and wait outside. / So, you come here often? Cute button.
jokes.setup: Michael ushers Lindsay away from the desperate hospital man she's already flirting with
jokes.punchline: As they leave, Lindsay immediately deploys a pickup line ('So, you come here often?') on the grieving hospital man — not missing a beat
jokes.explanation: The hospital-pickup-line is exactly as inappropriate as it should be, and 'cute button' adds a specific, weird detail that elevates it beyond a generic bad-taste joke. Very Lindsay.

jokes.text: Oh, the red tape. / Looks a little bit more like Red Prawn, Dad. It's the shrimp fest.
jokes.setup: George Sr. arrives in a conspicuous lobster-colored bib/costume from Shrimp Fest, explaining his delay as 'red tape'
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects that it looks more like 'Red Prawn' — a visual pun connecting the red bib to the phrase 'red tape'
jokes.explanation: A wordplay/visual pun that works on multiple levels simultaneously — 'red tape' as bureaucratic delay, the physical red prawn bib as the visual joke, and Michael's deadpan correction landing the punchline. Economy is superb. One of the cleaner wordplay moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Right now, he doesn't know who B. Bluth is.
jokes.setup: George Sr. asks if Buster knows who N. Bluth is
jokes.punchline: Michael's response that Buster currently doesn't even know he himself is 'B. Bluth' — the coma has wiped his basic self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy joke — two proper nouns and one letter swap do all the comedic lifting. The pivot from N. Bluth to B. Bluth recontextualizes the mystery arc as a joke on Buster's comatose state. Sharp and fast.

jokes.text: You're the lawyer? / That's the same representation Captain Hook had.
jokes.setup: Michael announces he'll be pretending to be their attorney at the mock trial 'before Judge Reinhold'
jokes.punchline: Lucille makes the Captain Hook connection again — Buster played Captain Hook in a school play; now this situation mirrors his
jokes.explanation: Second callback to the Captain Hook running gag, delivered by Lucille rather than the narrator this time. The joke deepens — now we also know Buster-as-Captain-Hook was also poorly 'represented' (without a hand). Solid callback, gains from context.

jokes.text: It's so good to laugh again. Feels good. It does. / They say seven minutes heals all wounds.
jokes.setup: The family is laughing about Buster's coma/Captain Hook situation while Buster lies in the hospital
jokes.punchline: George Sr. mangles 'time heals all wounds' as 'seven minutes heals all wounds' — both the wrong number and a hilariously short timeline for healing
jokes.explanation: The malapropism of 'seven minutes' for 'time' is a beautifully specific wrong number — not 'a few minutes' but exactly seven. Also layers dark comedy: the family healing in seven minutes from their son/brother's coma.

jokes.text: Wait, not the Judge Reinhold show from all those magazine ads? / They're all over the place. Look at this. / My name is Judge.
jokes.setup: George Sr. recognizes the Judge Reinhold show from 'talking magazine ads'
jokes.punchline: Gob produces a talking magazine ad that plays the 'My name is Judge' clip — the gag of a magazine with an embedded audio chip is absurdist tech humor
jokes.explanation: The talking magazine is a good absurdist background gag — it's treated as cutting-edge technology everyone is amazed by, which grounds the satire of reality TV and media excess.

jokes.text: I could use this in my ventriloquism act.
jokes.setup: Gob plays the talking magazine ad for the Judge Reinhold show
jokes.punchline: Gob's immediate reaction is to see the talking magazine as a prop for his ventriloquist act — he is always performing, always looking for the angle
jokes.explanation: This becomes a payoff later in the episode (he does use it). One line, total character essence. Rewatch bonus because it's foreshadowing disguised as a throwaway.

jokes.text: Well, Buster's in what we call a light-to-no-coma. In layman's terms, it might be considered a very heavy nap.
jokes.setup: Doctor gives the family a medical update on Buster's condition
jokes.punchline: 'Light-to-no-coma' is a hilariously non-committal medical diagnosis; 'very heavy nap' is the comedically useless layman's translation
jokes.explanation: 'A very heavy nap' as a medical description is one of the episode's most quotable lines. The escalating hedge ('light-to-no-coma') is immediately undercut by the hilariously unhelpful simplification. Strong originality — this specific phrasing is genuinely inventive.

jokes.text: Could that mean that he's faking it? / Highly likely this is fake, although there doesn't seem to be very much brain activity. / No, that's him.
jokes.setup: Doctor suggests the coma may be fake; Michael pushes back
jokes.punchline: Narrator deadpans 'No, that's him' — confirming that the low brain activity is Buster's baseline, not evidence of a coma
jokes.explanation: 'No, that's him' — three words, perfect timing, devastating. The narrator's interjection overrides the entire medical conversation with a damning character assessment of Buster's intelligence. A high-water mark for the show's narrator-as-comedian technique. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: I don't care if it takes from now till the end of Shrimp Fest.
jokes.setup: George Sr. pledges to stay at the hospital as long as it takes
jokes.punchline: His devotion is measured not in open-ended time but in Shrimp Fest's duration — a festival that has been running throughout and defines his priorities
jokes.explanation: Character-perfect line — George Sr.'s paternal devotion is undermined by the horizon being defined by a shrimp festival. Shows his priorities in a single sentence.

jokes.text: Well, we have a group of young people who put on fake weddings for our Alzheimer's patients.
jokes.setup: Hospital volunteer offers activities for George Michael to do
jokes.punchline: The listed activity is performing fake weddings for Alzheimer's patients — presented as wholesome volunteerism
jokes.explanation: The premise of fake weddings for dementia patients as both dark and earnest charity work is exactly the show's sweet spot. Sets up the whole George Michael/Maeby subplot payoff.

jokes.text: Would I have to touch the old people? / Only their hearts.
jokes.setup: George Michael asks if he'd have to physically touch the elderly patients
jokes.punchline: Coordinator's 'Only their hearts' — a sincere, saccharine response that is also funny because it dodges the question while being slightly pretentious
jokes.explanation: Competent setup-punchline. The coordinator's response is too sincere to be a great joke on its own, but works as a tone-setter for the bit.

jokes.text: I don't have a girlfriend. / Sister, then. Or a cousin.
jokes.setup: Coordinator tells George Michael to bring his girlfriend to be the bride; GM says he doesn't have one
jokes.punchline: Coordinator casually suggests 'Sister, then. Or a cousin.' — a suggestion that the show knows is loaded given George Michael's feelings for his cousin Maeby
jokes.explanation: The joke is enormous in context. A well-meaning coordinator accidentally suggests the precise person George Michael has inappropriate feelings for. 'Or a cousin' is the punchline the show has been building toward for multiple seasons. Rewatch bonus because knowing the context makes it hit twice as hard. Strong character integration (would only be this funny with George Michael in this position).

jokes.text: Guess who liked that idea? / He says he doesn't want to do it. I'm actually trying to respect his choices lately.
jokes.setup: Narrator sets up the rhetorical question 'Guess who liked that idea?'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. — the obvious answer — immediately volunteers that George Michael 'doesn't want to do it,' but then a beat later it's clear George Michael absolutely wants to
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'Guess who liked that idea?' sets up George Michael's obvious enthusiasm, then George Sr.'s protective intervention ('he says he doesn't want to') followed by 'I'm actually trying to respect his choices lately' is a fine Arrested Development layering.

jokes.text: I mean, the girl I would want to give, and she'll want to give, too. And we'll do that together. It's for them, for the sake of... I just... It's a great day... For being sad.
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to explain — without saying Maeby's name — why he wants to do the fake wedding
jokes.punchline: His sentence completely collapses under the weight of his own barely-suppressed feelings — ending with the inexplicable 'It's a great day... for being sad'
jokes.explanation: 'It's a great day for being sad' is a genuinely great comedy line — it's George Michael's brain short-circuiting under emotional overload. The increasingly disintegrating sentence structure before this is expert cringe comedy, and the non sequitur landing is pitch perfect. Very specific to Michael Cera's George Michael performance style.

jokes.text: So, I've got a date with the guy with the brain-dead mom. I mean, it's just a picket for them to pull the plug on our loved ones.
jokes.setup: Lindsay reports to Michael about her new 'date'
jokes.punchline: She's dating a man she met at the hospital whose goal is to end his mother's life — and Lindsay is packaging this as normal romantic activity
jokes.explanation: Lindsay has converted a meeting at her comatose brother's bedside into a date to help someone unplug their dying mother. Per-word dark comedy yield is high. Strong Lindsay characterization.

jokes.text: Given up hope on Buster pretty quickly there, Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Lindsay explains she's going on a 'pull the plug' protest date while Buster is in a coma
jokes.punchline: Michael's dry observation that she's already given up on their comatose brother
jokes.explanation: Competent straight-man observation. Works but doesn't surprise.

jokes.text: I love soup. If the only thing I could do was lay in bed all day and eat soup, I'd be happy. I wouldn't even have to taste it. I could just take it through a tube. That would actually be better, 'cause I wouldn't even burn my mouth.
jokes.setup: Lindsay claims to know what Buster wants and that he wants to die
jokes.punchline: Buster delivers a sincere, enthusiastic vision of his ideal life that is literally the exact conditions of his current coma — and finds them desirable
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Buster describes his dream life — bed-ridden, tube-fed, no sensory stimulation — and the audience realizes this is his current comatose state, which he apparently engineered (or stumbled into) as an improvement. The escalation from 'I love soup' to 'I wouldn't even have to burn my mouth' is perfectly constructed. 'Never let me die' is the killer punchline — he's arguing against Lindsay's position using his own oblivious contentment. Near-perfect character integration; only Buster Bluth can deliver this monologue.

jokes.text: Never let me die.
jokes.setup: Buster has just described his ideal life as unconscious, tube-fed, bed-ridden
jokes.punchline: 'Never let me die' — simultaneously the punchline to his soup speech and a complete misreading of what his sister is plotting
jokes.explanation: Three words. 'Never let me die.' Buster has described wanting to be tube-fed in a bed forever, then punctuates it with the absolute conviction of 'never let me die' — directly contradicting Lindsay's plan and simultaneously revealing he is living his ideal life in a coma. Maximum economy (10 is appropriate — literally three words carry all the weight), perfect structure as the final beat of the soup bit, complete character specificity. This is what a 9+ economy score looks like.

jokes.text: You'll call me up to the stand, say something like, 'Who is this little friend?' And he'll say... / My name is Judge. / Whose name is Judge? / My name is. / Okay. / That's a silly name. / That's enough. / Judge my name. / Yes, I am judging your name. It am silly. / Is. / Please stop. / Now you're correcting my grammar?
jokes.setup: Gob pitches his courtroom ventriloquism bit to Michael
jokes.punchline: The exchange rapidly escalates from the 'My name is Judge' joke into Franklin correcting Gob's grammar, which then spirals into Gob correcting Franklin back — it's a loop of absurdism
jokes.explanation: The escalating Gob/Franklin grammar debate is a showcase for Gob's total inability to control his own performance. Franklin taking over — correcting Gob's grammar — and Gob correcting Franklin back is meta-absurdism: a puppet has corrected its puppeteer. 'It am silly' / 'Is' is a precise, funny beat.

jokes.text: Looks like there's a secret room in this one, off the hallway. Wonder if we've got that. I'm gonna go check that out.
jokes.setup: Michael studies the Iraqi blueprint and notices a secret room
jokes.punchline: His casual, curious tone about potentially having a secret room in his home — foreshadowing he is about to find exactly that
jokes.explanation: Straightforward AD dramatic irony — Michael is curious whether the secret room exists right before he discovers it. Rewatch bonus because the nonchalance is funnier knowing what he's about to find.

jokes.text: Nichael Bluth? / Am I N. Bluth?
jokes.setup: Michael finds his birth certificate in the secret room
jokes.punchline: His name is 'Nichael' — a typo on the birth certificate that makes him 'N. Bluth,' the mystery witness
jokes.explanation: The episode's central mystery resolved as a pun/typo joke is an AD signature move — the dramatic reveal deflated into wordplay. 'Nichael' is a perfectly absurd not-quite-name. The structure is excellent: a season's worth of N. Bluth tension, resolved by one dropped letter. High rewatch bonus — the Lucille slip ('she') is now retroactively funnier AND mysterious (there IS another N. Bluth, a woman, revealed at episode's end).

jokes.text: And for some reason this stack of bodybuilding magazines. / Well, I guess your dad must've had these from way back when, huh? / My name is... / That seems kind of current.
jokes.setup: Michael and Tobias find the secret room; Tobias deflects the bodybuilding magazines as George Sr.'s old collection
jokes.punchline: One of the talking Judge Reinhold magazine ads plays — 'My name is' — revealing the magazines are current (and Tobias's)
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke: (1) Tobias is caught with current bodybuilding magazines, revealing his vanity/closet, (2) the talking magazine spontaneously betrays him, (3) Michael's 'that seems kind of current' is perfectly dry. Three jokes in four lines.

jokes.text: Who am I trying to kid, Michael? I was trying to beef up to impress that wife of mine. But now she's after some rich guy who's trying to kill his mom.
jokes.setup: Tobias is caught with bodybuilding magazines
jokes.punchline: He confesses he was bulking up for Lindsay — who is now dating the man trying to pull his mother's feeding tube
jokes.explanation: Tobias's romantic frustration converging with Lindsay's dark-comedy date is an efficient subplot collision. 'Trying to kill his mom' said with such casual resignation is the kicker.

jokes.text: One room over, George Michael was also trying to convince someone to pursue a fake marriage.
jokes.setup: Tobias announces he will fight for his marriage with Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Narrator cuts to George Michael pursuing the Alzheimer's fake wedding with Maeby — drawing a parallel between a struggling adult marriage and a teenage fake one, both described identically
jokes.explanation: The narrator juxtaposition — Tobias fighting for his fake-real marriage while George Michael pursues a real-fake one — is an elegant structural joke. AD's narration-as-comedy device at its best: the cut is the punchline.

jokes.text: We'd have to kiss. Wouldn't that be kind of weird after what we've been through? / Well, that was a long time ago. / It had been two months since they'd kissed.
jokes.setup: Maeby raises the awkwardness of kissing George Michael (her cousin) for the fake wedding
jokes.punchline: George Michael dismisses it as 'a long time ago'; Narrator reveals it was two months — which is both (a) not long at all and (b) the show noting they did kiss
jokes.explanation: The narrator undercuts George Michael's 'long time ago' with the precise counter: 'two months.' The joke is that George Michael desperately wants this to be less weird than it is, and the narrator refuses to let him. 'Two months' is the perfect number — long enough to try to dismiss, short enough to be ridiculous.

jokes.text: And so, like many people who no longer felt anything for each other, they decided to get married.
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby agree to do the fake wedding together
jokes.punchline: Narrator frames the fake wedding using the language of jaded real marriages — 'like many people who no longer felt anything for each other' — which is both darkly funny about marriage AND completely wrong (George Michael feels everything)
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The narrator's framing is simultaneously a cynical joke about real marriage, a perfect ironic inversion (George Michael is overwhelmed with feeling), and a darkly funny observation about how the show treats its own romantic subplot. 'Like many people who no longer felt anything for each other' is quotable in isolation as a marriage observation, and it's doubly funny in context. Very high scores across the board are justified.

jokes.text: I'm N. Bluth, aren't I? Legally, that's my real name.
jokes.setup: Michael confronts his father about the Nichael birth certificate
jokes.punchline: George Sr. confirms 'Legally, that's my real name' — meaning Michael has been 'Nichael' his whole life
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the Nichael joke — now confirmed as legal fact. Less punchy than the initial discovery but necessary for the plot continuation.

jokes.text: Close to Christmas. Was that hard?
jokes.setup: George Sr. notes the birth certificate has Michael's birthday wrong
jokes.punchline: Michael confirms 'that is my birthday'; George Sr. immediately pivots to 'Close to Christmas. Was that hard?' — ignoring the legal crisis to give a pat, vaguely dismissive consolation about Christmas birthdays
jokes.explanation: The abrupt pivot from 'your birth certificate might be sending you to prison' to 'close to Christmas — tough huh?' is a George Sr. special. It's deflection as character revelation — he changes the subject to something trivial with the confidence of a man who has never taken accountability. 'Was that hard?' is a weirdly specific and therefore funnier question than 'that must have been hard.'

jokes.text: Why can't you be like Buster? He put himself in a coma to protect this family, poor kid.
jokes.setup: George Sr. scolds Michael for not being more cooperative/loyal
jokes.punchline: His ideal of loyalty is Buster accidentally roofie-ing himself into a coma — held up as an aspirational act of family devotion
jokes.explanation: George Sr. holding up accidental self-roofie-induced coma as the gold standard of familial loyalty is a perfect encapsulation of this family's dysfunction. 'Poor kid' as an afterthought is the pity-kicker. Very George Sr.

jokes.text: Shrimp Fest is over, Pop. / Really? Better go to Chili's.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to get George Sr. to leave the hospital and focus
jokes.punchline: George Sr. interprets 'Shrimp Fest is over' not as a life-lesson metaphor but as an actual update on the festival — and his immediate contingency plan is Chili's
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. Michael uses 'Shrimp Fest is over' as a metaphor; George Sr. hears only shrimp news and pivots directly to 'Better go to Chili's.' The specificity of 'Chili's' — not a vague restaurant but exactly this chain — is character comedy gold. George Sr.'s priorities crystallized in two words. High quotability.

jokes.text: All they're doing in there is trying to keep people alive. It's a life mill.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has joined a protest outside the hospital
jokes.punchline: 'Life mill' — Lindsay's outrage that a hospital keeps people alive, framed as an industrial negative
jokes.explanation: 'Life mill' is a genuinely brilliant coinage — it's funny as an invented term, funny as Lindsay logic (hospitals are bad because they keep people alive), and perfectly economical. Only Lindsay Fünke would be indignant at a hospital for doing its job. One of the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: And he talked me into protesting, too. Show me your sign. / [Tobias shows 'PULL THE PLUG' on one side] / No, the other side.
jokes.setup: Tobias joins the protest; Lindsay asks to see his sign
jokes.punchline: The 'other side' of the sign implies a second, presumably more embarrassing or contradictory message — a visual gag where the sign's content is the joke
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that works in the show's single-camera format — the audience sees what's on the other side of the sign. The setup is efficient; impact depends entirely on what the other side says (inferred from context to be something self-defeating or embarrassing for Tobias).

jokes.text: Now, why don't we leave the fruits with the vegetables, and go get a coffee?
jokes.setup: Lindsay wants to separate Tobias from the protest (and Father Ben from Tobias)
jokes.punchline: 'Leave the fruits with the vegetables' — ostensibly referring to protesters (idle people) but 'fruits' is also a slur-pun on Tobias's implied homosexuality, and 'vegetables' refers to the comatose patients inside
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered wordplay that works on three levels simultaneously: (1) idle protesters as 'fruits and vegetables,' (2) 'fruits' as a pointed comment on Tobias's sexuality/identity, (3) 'vegetables' as hospital patients in comas. The pun density in one natural-sounding sentence is impressive. Very AD. High quotability.

jokes.text: And Maeby did the thing she had always thought was so corny... / I can't do this. I can't do this. / ...and ran away.
jokes.setup: Narrator says Maeby did 'the thing she always thought was so corny' — implying she did something sentimental (kissed George Michael)
jokes.punchline: She ran away from the altar — the 'corny' thing was the runaway bride trope, which she had specifically said she didn't want (from their earlier conversation about making a 'wedding film')
jokes.explanation: Structural perfection — the narrator builds to a fake-out, letting you think Maeby did something tender, then the punchline is that she enacted the exact trope she'd mocked. Callbacks to Maeby's earlier dialogue about the 'corny runaway bride bit.' The narrator's framing does all the work. One of the episode's best structural jokes.

jokes.text: What are we gonna do? / Just give it a minute.
jokes.setup: Maeby has run away; George Michael is left at the fake altar
jokes.punchline: Officiant tells him to 'just give it a minute' — normalizing the runaway bride as a predictable delay
jokes.explanation: 'Just give it a minute' implies this happens so often at Alzheimer's weddings that there's an established wait protocol. Dark and efficiently funny.

jokes.text: Now it's a bar mitzvah.
jokes.setup: The Alzheimer's fake wedding has collapsed; Maeby ran away; George Michael is left alone
jokes.punchline: A beat later, the officiant announces 'Now it's a bar mitzvah' — the ceremony has pivoted to a completely different religious rite with no explanation
jokes.explanation: Four words, absurdist perfection. The transition from failed wedding to bar mitzvah is logically impossible and requires zero explanation — the Alzheimer's patients won't notice, and neither does the officiant seem to care. 'Now it's a bar mitzvah' is the platonic ideal of absurdist comedy economy. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: And as George Michael acted like a boy becoming a man, Michael returned to his office to find a man acting like a boy.
jokes.setup: Bar mitzvah pivot / scene transition back to Michael's office
jokes.punchline: The narrator mirrors the two situations — boy becoming a man (bar mitzvah), man acting like a boy (Gob) — with structural symmetry that's the joke itself
jokes.explanation: AD's narrator at its most elegant — the chiasmic sentence structure ('boy becoming a man / man acting like a boy') is funny in itself, and the double-reveal (George Michael's bar mitzvah, Gob's petulance) makes it a perfect transition joke. This is the kind of writing that defines the show.

jokes.text: What's this? / Just practising my testimony in a fake witness box. / And you couldn't have just taken my stuff off the desk first or unplugged my computer?
jokes.setup: Michael returns to his office; Gob has set up a fake witness box on his desk
jokes.punchline: Michael's complaint is not 'why do you have a fake witness box' but the eminently reasonable 'couldn't you have moved my stuff first' — normalizing the absurdity while highlighting it
jokes.explanation: Michael's complaint is the perfect straight-man response — not addressing the absurdity but the practical inconsideration. Good character integration for both Michael (reasonable) and Gob (oblivious).

jokes.text: Check this out. You'll say, 'Who's this little friend?' And I'll say, 'Well, he's not really feeling that well.' And you'll say, 'Oh, what's wrong with the little guy?' And he'll say... / 'Patient presented with gastric bacterial infection.'
jokes.setup: Gob pitches his new improved Franklin ventriloquism bit using the stolen medical recording
jokes.punchline: Franklin recites a medical chart verbatim — a talking puppet delivering a clinical diagnosis as his personality
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Gob's Franklin scheme is great — each new plan is worse than the last. The specific medical language recited by a puppet is absurdist comedy with strong internal logic (Gob genuinely thinks this is a good act).

jokes.text: Wow. Sounds like you could use some tricycling.
jokes.setup: Franklin/Gob delivers the 'tetracycline should not be administered' medical line
jokes.punchline: Michael mishears or twists 'tetracycline' to 'tricycling' — a pun on the antibiotic name
jokes.explanation: A quick wordplay beat — 'tetracycline' → 'tricycling.' The joke is Michael's attempt at a dry response to a truly absurd situation. Efficient wordplay.

jokes.text: Your lips are still moving, and you stole the doctor's tape recorder?
jokes.setup: Gob's new improved Franklin act uses a stolen medical tape recorder
jokes.punchline: Two complaints in one sentence: (1) the lips are still moving (the original problem, unresolved), (2) he stole the tape (new escalating crime)
jokes.explanation: The callback to the lip-moving gag plus the escalation to tape theft in a single sentence is efficient. Works as a running gag payoff while adding a new crime.

jokes.text: I need this! My career needs this.
jokes.setup: Michael takes back the stolen tape; Gob protests
jokes.punchline: 'My career needs this' — Gob believes a stolen medical recording played through a puppet is his career breakthrough
jokes.explanation: 'My career needs this' is quintessential Gob self-delusion delivered with complete conviction. The two-sentence structure is tight and the second line ('my career needs this') lands as both pathetic and funny.

jokes.text: You know what? Dad was right. If you really cared about this family, you'd be more supportive of me in this. / Gob, I'm not gonna turn this mock trial into some kind of... / You were gonna say 'mockery,' weren't you? / I was in trouble, like, three words into that.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob he won't turn the mock trial into something else
jokes.punchline: Gob anticipates 'mockery' as the next word — which would have made 'mock trial' → 'mockery,' a pun Gob catches before Michael. Michael admits he was 'in trouble three words into that sentence.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The joke operates on two levels: (1) the wordplay of 'mock trial' / 'mockery' is a genuine pun, and (2) Gob — usually the least self-aware character — catches it before Michael, the straight man. Michael's admission 'I was in trouble, like, three words into that' is perfectly calibrated self-aware comedy. The meta-awareness of the language trap is AD writing at its peak density. Very high scores across the board are warranted.

jokes.text: Read the Patriot Act. Anything said in a mock trial or daytime courtroom show can be used in any real criminal proceeding or primetime procedural show, subject to the interpretation of the presiding judge or the executive producer.
jokes.setup: Wayne explains why the mock trial matters legally
jokes.punchline: The Patriot Act apparently covers both mock trials AND daytime courtroom shows, with equivalence drawn between 'presiding judge' and 'executive producer'
jokes.explanation: Media satire done with mock-legal precision. The parallel between 'presiding judge' and 'executive producer' collapses reality TV and the justice system elegantly. 'Subject to the interpretation of the executive producer' is the kicker.

jokes.text: In our case, Judge Reinhold is both. / Did he get E.P.? / Sure did. / Good for him.
jokes.setup: Wayne notes Judge Reinhold presides over both court and the show
jokes.punchline: Michael's response isn't shock at the legal implications — it's a genuine congratulations that Judge Reinhold got an executive producer credit. 'Good for him.' Sincere and absurd.
jokes.explanation: 'Good for him.' — three words of perfect sincerity in a moment that calls for alarm. Michael's genuine pleasure at Reinhold's E.P. credit amid a conversation about legal jeopardy is quintessential AD straight-man absurdism. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's my dad's exercise room. / Although for all her worldliness, Maeby was still a child in many ways.
jokes.setup: Maeby has fled to the secret room; George Michael finds her and says it's his dad's exercise room
jokes.punchline: Narrator notes Maeby — who routinely runs movie studios and scams adults — 'was still a child in many ways,' implying emotional immaturity about the relationship
jokes.explanation: The irony is sharp — 'worldly' Maeby is rattled by a fake wedding. Narrator's observation works as both character commentary and gentle irony on the cousin relationship. Good economy.

jokes.text: Well, you know, the Torah tells us that the larger wrong is to put our own feelings before the commitments we've made. You know, towards the sick, aged and gross. / That's not in the Torah.
jokes.setup: George Michael cites 'the Torah' to argue Maeby should go back to the fake wedding
jokes.punchline: 'That's not in the Torah' — Maeby's flat, certain rebuttal confirms George Michael invented this scripture
jokes.explanation: George Michael invoking fabricated Torah passages to pressure his cousin into a fake wedding is a rich comedic concept. 'Towards the sick, aged and gross' as his specific invented amendment is very funny, and Maeby's unfazed 'that's not in the Torah' is the clean rebuttal. Strong character integration — George Michael's earnest desperation to use any leverage is pitch perfect.

jokes.text: And I just feel now like we left a lot of old people sad there today. / Again, not true.
jokes.setup: George Michael claims they left the Alzheimer's patients sad
jokes.punchline: Narrator interrupts with 'Again, not true' — having already converted the wedding to a bar mitzvah, the patients are apparently fine
jokes.explanation: 'Again, not true' — the narrator's curt contradiction is three words that also callback to the bar mitzvah scene, implying the Alzheimer's patients cheerfully pivoted to a new religion. The 'again' suggests this isn't the first time George Michael has been factually wrong in this conversation. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: I'm just saying maybe we could take those weird feelings and turn them into something positive. / He really wanted that kiss.
jokes.setup: George Michael delivers an earnest speech about channeling 'weird feelings' into something positive
jokes.punchline: Narrator cuts through the entire speech with 'He really wanted that kiss' — reducing the whole elaborate justification to its true motive
jokes.explanation: The narrator demolishing George Michael's elaborate rationalization with a single sentence ('He really wanted that kiss') is perfectly timed and devastating. This is the narrator's function as truth-teller at its best — cutting through adolescent self-justification with clinical clarity. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Hey, you guys know about this room, too, huh? I'm just looking for some evidence for my trial against Pop-pop. / For Pop-pop.
jokes.setup: Michael walks in on Maeby and George Michael in the secret room
jokes.punchline: Michael says he's looking for evidence 'against Pop-pop'; narrator immediately corrects 'for Pop-pop' — Michael's Freudian slip reveals his real instinct (to build a case against his father)
jokes.explanation: The narrator catching Michael's Freudian slip in real time — 'For Pop-pop' — is the narrator at its most active and intrusive. Two words cut Michael's entire defense posture. Strong economy, strong character integration (Michael's ambivalent feelings toward his father are a series-long theme).

jokes.text: I'm trying to get on with my life, and you know what? You can't deal with it. / No, I can't, Lindsay. You're not interested in me. And you're not capable of intimacy. / Well, just fake it! / Coma. / And believe it or not, that actually got through to them.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay are arguing in Buster's hospital room about their relationship
jokes.punchline: 'Well, just fake it!' — followed by the narrator's one-word observation 'Coma' and then 'that actually got through to them' — their argument ends because Buster's 'fake it' situation is right in front of them
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes. The word 'fake' triggers the narrator's 'Coma' as a single-word callback that connects their relationship problems to Buster's situation, and 'that actually got through to them' is an unexpected resolution — the visual of their comatose brother ends the argument. Beautifully constructed. The episode title 'Fakin It' pays off in this moment.

jokes.text: Now, will you please turn on the Cartoon Network and get the hell out of here.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay have been arguing at Buster's bedside, apparently moved by the 'faking it' realization
jokes.punchline: Buster (apparently not as unconscious as believed) breaks his silence to request Cartoon Network and dismiss them — simultaneously revealing he heard everything and that his priorities are cartoons
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Buster is conscious — and his first words are to demand Cartoon Network — is a complete character encapsulation. 'Get the hell out of here' from someone who was supposedly comatose has perfect comic timing. His priorities (cartoons > his sister's marital crisis > his medical condition) are exactly right. High quotability.

jokes.text: All rise for acting's highest honour, Judge Reinhold.
jokes.setup: Court is called to order for the mock trial
jokes.punchline: 'Acting's highest honour' — simultaneously the standard court introduction ('all rise') and a reference to Judge Reinhold as an actor, with 'acting' doing double work (legal proceedings / the craft of acting)
jokes.explanation: 'Acting's highest honour' is a beautifully compressed pun — it works as court ceremony (courtroom 'acting' / proceedings) and as a reference to the actor Judge Reinhold. The double meaning is precise and earned. One of the episode's better wordplay moments.

jokes.text: Wow, thank you. That's the first time I ever got a standing ovation before the show. / They're not clapping. / They don't in a courtroom.
jokes.setup: Judge Reinhold mistakes the 'all rise' as a standing ovation
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects him: 'They're not clapping.' Judge Reinhold: 'They don't in a courtroom.' — His rationalization for why silent rising is still an ovation
jokes.explanation: 'They don't in a courtroom' is the punchline's punchline — Judge Reinhold retrofits his misinterpretation into a logical framework, which is funnier than if he'd just been confused. The three-line exchange is tight and efficient.

jokes.text: Well, I spend so much time making sweet love on my wife that it's hard to hear anything over the clatter of her breasts and...
jokes.setup: Tobias is on the mock-trial stand, asked if he ever heard family members discuss Iraq
jokes.punchline: His answer is a TMI digression about his sex life, using the absurd phrase 'clatter of her breasts' — grotesque, oversharing, and almost certainly untrue given their relationship
jokes.explanation: 'The clatter of her breasts' is a wildly specific and terrible phrase that reveals Tobias's performative machismo — he's overcorrecting on heterosexuality in a public legal setting. Given the show's established reading of Tobias, the over-compensation makes it funnier. 'Making sweet love on my wife' (not 'to') is also a telling grammatical error. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Your witness. / I have nothing.
jokes.setup: Tobias's testimony has been so strange and off-topic that the prosecutor passes immediately
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I have nothing' — as the defense attorney, having nothing to add after his own witness's testimony is a complete failure
jokes.explanation: Two words that summarize Michael's entire legal incompetence. 'I have nothing' as a reaction to his own witness's testimony is perfectly calibrated. The economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: How should I know where the money comes from? I just know where it goes. To taking care of my poor comatose son.
jokes.setup: Lucille is on the stand, questioned about Bluth Company finances
jokes.punchline: Her defense is aggressive ignorance of income + knowledge of expenses, then pivots to emotional manipulation ('my poor comatose son') — a masterclass in Lucille testimony
jokes.explanation: Lucille's courtroom strategy — plausible deniability about income, weaponizing family sympathy — is a perfect encapsulation of her character. 'My poor comatose son' as the emotional pivot is exactly what Lucille would do, and she would do it without blinking.

jokes.text: I have no cross at this time. / Well, this is why you're not handling my real defence.
jokes.setup: Michael doesn't cross-examine his own clients after their disastrous testimony
jokes.punchline: George Sr. — who just admitted he's saving emotional manipulation for the real trial — criticizes Michael's lawyering
jokes.explanation: The irony of George Sr. criticizing Michael's lawyering seconds after revealing his own manipulation strategy is good situational comedy. Works within context.

jokes.text: Well, listen, have fun out there, because this is the last time you're gonna see this side of a courtroom till your second wife divorces your freckled ass.
jokes.setup: George Sr. trash-talks Michael before he does his witness examination
jokes.punchline: George Sr. predicts Michael will get divorced (noting his 'freckled ass' specifically) — a weirdly specific insult that reveals George Sr.'s particular contempt
jokes.explanation: 'Your freckled ass' is the kind of specific, weird insult that only a neglectful/contemptuous father would deliver. The prediction of a second divorce (implying a future marriage Michael doesn't know about) is also darkly specific. Good character-comedy nastiness.

jokes.text: Look at that. You just blew your wedding speech.
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just hurled insults at Michael in a courtroom
jokes.punchline: Michael notes that George Sr. has 'blown his wedding speech' — framing the father-son hostility as a wasted opportunity for future sentimentality
jokes.explanation: A fast, clean comeback that uses the 'second wife' setup from George Sr.'s insult. 'You just blew your wedding speech' recontextualizes the hostility as a waste of future sentiment. Sharp and quick.

jokes.text: State your name. / My name is Judge. / What kind of name is that, magazine?
jokes.setup: Gob brings Franklin to the stand; the Judge asks Franklin's name
jokes.punchline: Judge Reinhold's response to 'My name is Judge' is 'What kind of name is that, magazine?' — connecting Franklin's name to the talking magazine ads where he's heard this before
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the 'My name is Judge' talking magazine gag in the actual courtroom — and Judge Reinhold dismissively says 'what kind of name is that, magazine?' — collapsing the real Judge's name and the magazine into one confused exchange. Very satisfying structural payoff.

jokes.text: I'm just gonna go ahead and jump in right here. / I have nothing for him.
jokes.setup: Franklin on the stand is going badly; the prosecutor jumps in
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately folds: 'I have nothing for him' — mirroring his earlier 'I have nothing' and confirming his complete failure as an attorney
jokes.explanation: Second instance of 'I have nothing' — works as a running gag on Michael's legal incompetence, slightly less punchy than the first time.

jokes.text: Well, listen, have fun out there, because this is the last time you're gonna see this side of a courtroom — Franklin? You're calling Franklin, Mikey? / I sure am, pal. I support my brother.
jokes.setup: Michael announces a surprise witness
jokes.punchline: Gob's excitement at Franklin being called is addressed to 'Mikey' — revealing Gob cares more about his puppet's legal career than his family's actual legal jeopardy; Michael's 'I support my brother' is sincere but also about helping Gob, not the case
jokes.explanation: Michael's 'I support my brother' is doing double-duty — it's sincere (he does support Gob) and also means he's using a puppet as a legal stratagem. The moment humanizes their relationship briefly.

jokes.text: Your Honour, this is highly unusual. / Well, I'm very curious to see where this is going. I'm gonna allow it. But I'm warning you, Mr Bluth, this better make for a good promo.
jokes.setup: Franklin (a puppet) is being sworn in as a legal witness
jokes.punchline: Judge Reinhold allows the puppet testimony but frames his ruling around the show's promotional value: 'this better make for a good promo'
jokes.explanation: 'This better make for a good promo' is a perfect meta-joke — Judge Reinhold is ruling as both judge and television executive producer. His legal reasoning is promotional value. Solid media satire.

jokes.text: Would you please press the play button on the tape recorder inside the puppet? / If you don't do it, we're gonna go after N. Bluth, and we think that's you.
jokes.setup: Michael has rigged Franklin with the stolen medical tape recorder containing the real prosecutor's threats
jokes.punchline: Franklin plays back Wayne's incriminating conversation with Michael — exposing the prosecution's improper pressure
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the entire episode's tape recorder subplot — what started as Gob stealing a medical tape to give Franklin 'dialogue' becomes Michael's actual legal strategy. The puppet delivering the exposé is structurally satisfying and funny. 'Would you please press the play button on the tape recorder inside the puppet?' is a uniquely Arrested Development sentence.

jokes.text: Why do there have to be puppets like Frank?
jokes.setup: Franklin has just played back Wayne's damaging audio threat in open court
jokes.punchline: Wayne's reaction is not legal alarm but an existential complaint about puppets like Franklin existing
jokes.explanation: 'Why do there have to be puppets like Frank?' is a genuinely funny response to prosecutorial exposure via ventriloquism. The complaint is about puppets, not about being caught — which is the absurdist kicker. Highly quotable as a standalone grievance.

jokes.text: Well, it looks like we've got a mistrial. / But on the plus side, we've also got a hung jury. Hit it!
jokes.setup: Judge Reinhold rules mistrial after the prosecution is exposed
jokes.punchline: 'We've also got a hung jury' — both a legal term AND a setup for 'Hit it!' which kicks off Franklin's musical number; 'hung jury' as double entendre with the puppet's performance
jokes.explanation: 'Hung jury' → puppet performance punchline is a good wordplay pivot. The rhythm of 'mistrial' → 'hung jury' → 'Hit it!' is well-timed. Gob using a legal outcome as a performance cue is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You did good out there, Michael. But it's just a mock trial. Why didn't you just take it to the grand jury?
jokes.setup: Michael is complimented by Wayne after the mistrial
jokes.punchline: Wayne asks why Michael didn't just go to the grand jury — implying Michael had the evidence all along and wasted everyone's time with the mock trial
jokes.explanation: The punchline of Michael's legal naivety — he went through the whole mock trial without knowing the obvious next step. Works as commentary on Michael's recurring 'lawyer' delusion.

jokes.text: We don't abandon each other. No one's closer than the Bluth family. / They were. / I now pronounce you husband and wife.
jokes.setup: Jan praises the Bluths for sticking together
jokes.punchline: Narrator's 'They were' — cutting to George Michael and Maeby accidentally getting actually married by a real priest
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest structural jokes. 'No one's closer than the Bluth family' + 'They were' + 'I now pronounce you husband and wife' is a triple-layer payoff: (1) the irony of the claim, (2) the confirmation of literal closeness (married cousins), (3) the dark comedy of the accidental marriage. The two-word narrator punchline ('They were') is near-perfect compression. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: For, as it turns out, the fake priest was called to a patient's room, and Father Ben filled in, not knowing it was fake. Congratulations. And that's how the kids got married.
jokes.setup: The fake Alzheimer's wedding was always supposed to be fake
jokes.punchline: A chain of circumstances (fake priest called away, real Franciscan fills in, real marriage conducted) results in the cousins being accidentally legally married
jokes.explanation: 'And that's how the kids got married' is one of the great sitcom narrator punchlines. The chain of coincidences that leads to cousins accidentally marrying is the dark absurdist escalation the entire subplot has been building toward. The narrator's matter-of-fact 'congratulations' before the bomb-drop is pitch perfect. Father Ben was introduced specifically to enable this payoff — the show was tracking this. High marks everywhere.

jokes.text: The mock trial with J. Reinhold leads to imitators. All rise. Bud Cort is now in session.
jokes.setup: Coda following the episode
jokes.punchline: 'Bud Cort is now in session' — a different celebrity-named judge has a copycat show, continuing the Judge Reinhold joke into a cultural trend
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Judge Reinhold's show has spawned imitators featuring other oddly-named celebrities. 'Bud Cort is now in session' is a good extension of the bit — Bud Cort (Harold and Maude actor) as a judge has the same absurd energy.

jokes.text: Hard to sit when you got someone's hand up your ass.
jokes.setup: 'Please be seated' is announced in the Bud Cort courtroom
jokes.punchline: Franklin (the puppet) delivers this line — a puppet complaining about having someone's hand up his ass is the bluntest possible meta-joke about ventriloquism
jokes.explanation: Franklin breaking the fourth wall of ventriloquism — complaining about the mechanics of his own existence — is a great joke. 'Hard to sit when you got someone's hand up your ass' is quotable, darkly self-aware, and specific to Franklin as a character. Strong economy.

jokes.text: And Michael discovers that he wasn't N. Bluth after all. / I have an older sister? / So much for not abandoning family.
jokes.setup: Episode appears to have resolved the N. Bluth mystery as a typo
jokes.punchline: Final reveal: N. Bluth is a real person — an older sister Michael didn't know about — and the narrator's 'so much for not abandoning family' ties it to the episode's theme while darkly judging George Sr.
jokes.explanation: The episode's final beat is exceptional. The Nichael typo joke was itself a misdirection — there IS a real N. Bluth, a daughter George Sr. abandoned. The narrator's final line 'so much for not abandoning family' closes the episode's theme with perfect irony: Michael spent the episode insisting the Bluths never abandon each other, and his father abandoned a child. Lucille's earlier 'she' slip (Joke #12) is retroactively confirmed as a real clue. The structure is near-perfect: setup (episode's family-loyalty theme), misdirection (Nichael typo), final reveal (abandoned sister), thematic punchline. One of the episode's best moments. High scores justified.

tmdb_overview: George, Sr. hires a new attorney who asks the Bluths to participate in a mock trial to help prepare them for the real thing. The attorney gets actor Judge Reinhold to preside over the mock case. Meanwhile, Buster fakes a coma to get out of testifying, and George Michael and Maeby participate in a mock wedding to entertain hospital patients. Back at the office, Michael discovers that he may have a long-lost sister named Nellie Bluth.
insight: Season 3's densest episode: 69 jokes in 28 minutes, anchored by escalating character absurdity.
